faqs.question: What is the difference between addition and multiplication?
faqs.answer: Addition can add any numbers together (like 3 + 7 + 2). Multiplication is a shortcut that only works when you are adding the SAME number again and again. Instead of writing 5+5+5+5, you can write 4 x 5 = 20. So multiplication is a faster way to do repeated addition of equal groups.

faqs.question: How do I know when to use multiplication in a word problem?
faqs.answer: Look for clues that tell you there are equal groups! Words like 'each', 'every', 'per', or 'groups of' usually mean multiplication. For example: 'Each box has 6 pencils. There are 5 boxes. How many pencils?' Here 'each box has 6' tells you equal groups, so you multiply: 5 x 6 = 30.

faqs.question: Why does 3 x 4 give the same answer as 4 x 3?
faqs.answer: Imagine an array of dots: 3 rows with 4 dots each (3 x 4 = 12). Now turn the page sideways - you see 4 rows with 3 dots each (4 x 3 = 12). The total dots did not change! This is called the commutative property - you can swap the numbers in multiplication and the answer stays the same.

faqs.question: What is the easiest way to learn times tables?
faqs.answer: Start with the easy ones! Table of 2 (just double the number), table of 5 (ends in 0 or 5), and table of 10 (add a zero). Then learn table of 9 using the digit trick (digits add to 9). For others, skip counting helps: for the table of 7, count 7, 14, 21, 28, 35... Say them aloud every day and you will memorise them!

faqs.question: Why does any number multiplied by 0 give 0?
faqs.answer: Because multiplying by 0 means you have zero groups. If you have 0 groups of 5 chocolates, you have no chocolates at all! 0 x 5 = 0. And 5 x 0 means 5 groups of 0 chocolates - each group is empty, so you still have nothing. Zero groups = nothing!

faqs.question: Why does any number multiplied by 1 give the same number?
faqs.answer: Because multiplying by 1 means you have just one group. If you have 1 group of 8 mangoes, you have 8 mangoes. 1 x 8 = 8. Nothing is added extra, nothing is taken away. One group of any number is just that number itself!

faqs.question: How do arrays help with multiplication?
faqs.answer: Arrays make multiplication easy to see! When you arrange 3 rows of 4 objects, you can count the total (12) and see that 3 x 4 = 12. You can also turn the array sideways to see that 4 x 3 = 12 too. Drawing arrays helps you understand multiplication before you memorise the tables.

faqs.question: Can I use multiplication for unequal groups?
faqs.answer: No! Multiplication only works when groups are EQUAL (same number in each group). If you have 3 baskets with 4, 6, and 2 fruits, the groups are unequal, so you must add: 4 + 6 + 2 = 12. But if all 3 baskets had 4 fruits each, then you can multiply: 3 x 4 = 12.

faqs.question: What is the trick for the 9 times table?
faqs.answer: Two great tricks! (1) The digits of each answer add up to 9: 9x2=18 (1+8=9), 9x3=27 (2+7=9), 9x4=36 (3+6=9). (2) Use your fingers! Hold up 10 fingers. For 9x3, fold down finger number 3 from the left. Count fingers before it (2) and after it (7). Answer = 27!

faqs.question: Where do we use multiplication in daily life?
faqs.answer: We use it all the time! Buying things at a shop (3 packets at Rs 10 each = 3 x 10 = Rs 30), counting legs on furniture (4 chairs x 4 legs = 16 legs), figuring out seats (5 rows x 8 seats = 40 seats), calculating pages read (7 days x 6 pages a day = 42 pages). Multiplication is everywhere!
